sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 3 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: LEFT, RIGHT, UP.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 13, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: The board has three regions: (1) top-left shows a target pattern with white block above purple block, (2) top-right has two selector buttons (white and purple) with an indicator bar, (3) main area has a purple block above a white block (inverted from target). The goal appears to be matching the target pattern.
- Plan: Try arrow keys to see if there's a navigable cursor, and try different combinations of selector + SPACE.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 12 of about 55 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Interesting! The arrow keys DO affect the bottom bar:
- Initial: 58 c's + 6 B's (first B at col 58)
- After LEFT: same (58 c's + 6 B's) - no change
- After RIGHT: 57 c's + 7 B's (first B at col 57) - one more B
- After UP: 54 c's + 10 B's (first B at col 54) - 4 more B's
So the bottom bar is a timer/progress bar that shrinks (more B's = less remaining). LEFT didn't change it, RIGHT added 1 B, UP added 4 B's. This confirms it's a timer that counts down with each action. Different actions consume different amounts.
But wait - LEFT didn't change the bar. That's strange. Let me look again:
- Before LEFT: first B at col 58
- After LEFT: first B at col 58 (no change)
- After RIGHT: first B at col 57
- After UP: first B at col 54
Hmm, LEFT didn't change the bar. That's odd. Maybe LEFT is a "free" action or it didn't register properly. Or maybe the bar only changes for certain actions.
Actually, looking more carefully at the counts:
- Initial state: 58 c's + 6 B's
- After LEFT: 58 c's + 6 B's (no change)
- After RIGHT: 57 c's + 7 B's (-1 c, +1 B)
- After UP: 54 c's + 10 B's (-3 c, +3 B)
Wait, that doesn't add up. After RIGHT: 57+7=64, OK. After UP: 54+10=64, OK. So RIGHT consumed 1 unit and UP consumed 3 units. LEFT consumed 0 units.
This is a timer bar. LEFT might be a "free" action or maybe it's meant to navigate without consuming time.
OK, let me step back and think about the actual puzzle. I've been spending too many actions on exploration. Let me reconsider:
The target pattern is white-above-purple. The main area has:
1. A framed block (currently white)
2. An unframed block (currently white 10×10)
What I know:
- Clicking white selector → framed block becomes white
- Clicking purple selector → framed block becomes purple
- SPACE with purple → unframed block splits: purple(top 5×10) + white(bottom 5×10)
- SPACE with white → unframed block merges: white 10×10
The target is white-above-purple. The unframed block when split is purple-above-white (opposite of target). 
What if the goal is to make the main area look like the target? That would mean:
- The framed block should be white (top color of target)
- The unframed block should be purple (bottom color of target)
Currently:
- Framed block: white ✓
- Unframed block: white ✗ (needs to be purple)
So I need to make the unframed block all purple. But SPACE with purple only makes the top half purple. How do I make the entire block purple?
Wait, what if I need to:
1. Select purple
2. SPACE (splits: purple top + white bottom)
3. Select purple again (framed block stays purple)
4. SPACE again (what happens?)
Or maybe:
1. Select purple
2. SPACE (splits: purple top + white bottom)
3. SPACE again with purple (maybe it colors the white part too?)
Let me try this sequence.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model updated: Arrow keys affect the bottom timer bar (RIGHT=-1, UP=-3, LEFT=0). The timer bar is just a countdown. I need to focus on the actual puzzle mechanics.
Plan: Try SPACE twice with purple selected to see if it progressively colors the unframed block.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Current state: framed white, unframed white 10x10
# Let me try: select purple, SPACE, SPACE to see progressive coloring

# Select purple
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 4, 'col': 43}])
print("After selecting purple:")
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for node in seg['nodes']:
    if node['pixels'] > 20 and node['color'] in ['W', 'p', 'g']:
        print(f"  id={node['id']} color={node['color']} pixels={node['pixels']} boundary={node['boundary']}")

# SPACE once
action(['SPACE'])
print("\nAfter SPACE #1:")
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for node in seg['nodes']:
    if node['pixels'] > 20 and node['color'] in ['W', 'p', 'g']:
        print(f"  id={node['id']} color={node['color']} pixels={node['pixels']} boundary={node['boundary']}")

# SPACE again
action(['SPACE'])
print("\nAfter SPACE #2:")
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for node in seg['nodes']:
    if node['pixels'] > 20 and node['color'] in ['W', 'p', 'g']:
        print(f"  id={node['id']} color={node['color']} pixels={node['pixels']} boundary={node['boundary']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
